title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
code: # Install from CRAN install.packages("ggadjustedforest")  # Or install the development version from GitHub # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("kriz98/gg_adjusted_forest")

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: building multivariable models causal inference, exposure interest causal estimand applies . coefficients adjusted covariates (confounders) can however misinterpreted presented together effect estimate interest. Reporting can even mislead readers, confounder coefficients identified causal model, susceptible absorbing collider bias, mediation pathways, artefacts depending causal structures (Hernán & Robins, Causal Inference: , 2020; Westreich & Greenland, J Epidemiol, 2013). STROBE guidelines (Vandenbroucke et al., 2007) recent work estimand framework (ICH E9(R1), 2019) emphasise reporting clearly distinguish target quantity nuisance parameters. ggadjustedforest operationalises principles fits models specify exposes effect interest forest plots associated tables, hiding confounder estimates design. additional feature exploratory data analysis also provided whereby cumulative addition covariates can investigated.

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: examples use rotterdam breast cancer dataset survival package — 2,982 primary breast cancer patients Rotterdam tumour bank. research question throughout whether hormonal therapy (hormon) affects survival recurrence, adjusting patient tumour characteristics.
code: library(ggadjustedforest) #> ggadjustedforest 0.1.1 -- Forest plots for exposure effects, hiding confounders by design. #> See `?gg_adjusted_forest` to get started. library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union data(cancer, package = "survival")  df <- rotterdam |>   transmute(     hormon = hormon,   # 1 = hormonal therapy, 0 = none     age    = age,     size   = size,     # tumour size (mm)     grade  = grade,    # tumour grade     nodes  = nodes,    # positive lymph nodes     er10   = er / 10,  # oestrogen receptor (fmol/10 l)     recur  = recur,    # recurrence (0/1)     death  = death,    # all-cause death (0/1)     rtime  = rtime,    # time to recurrence/censoring     dtime  = dtime     # time to death/censoring   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na()  covariates <- c("age", "size", "grade", "nodes", "er10")

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: pipe-friendly API puts data first function composes naturally |>: returned object three components: render plot:
code: result <- df |>   gg_adjusted_forest(     outcome    = "death",     exposure   = "hormon",     covariates = covariates,     model_type = "logistic",     title      = "Effect of Hormonal Therapy on Death (Logistic)"   ) result$table #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   model      estimate conf.low conf.high     p.value     n #>   <fct>         <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>       <dbl> <int> #> 1 Unadjusted    1.21     0.967     1.52  0.0935       2982 #> 2 Adjusted      0.499    0.381     0.652 0.000000411  2982 result$plot

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: Although generally recommended performing causal inference, sequential addition covariates may helpful building regression models predictive descriptive aims. , feature provided visualise effect estimate changes covariates added sequentially. may aid selection parsimonious model best fit recommended principles laid ‘R Health Data Science’ Ewen Harrison Riinu Pius. use feature, set cumulative = TRUE use cumulative_labels provide human-readable row names:  numeric table available downstream use:
code: result_cum <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data       = df,   outcome    = "death",   exposure   = "hormon",   covariates = covariates,   model_type = "logistic",   cumulative = TRUE,   cumulative_labels = c(     "Unadjusted"                       = "Unadjusted",     "+ age"                            = "+ Age",     "+ age + size"                     = "+ Tumour size",     "+ age + size + grade"             = "+ Grade",     "+ age + size + grade + nodes"     = "+ Lymph nodes",     "+ age + size + grade + nodes + er10" = "+ Oestrogen receptor"   ),   title = "Cumulative Adjustment: Hormonal Therapy on Death" ) result_cum$plot result_cum$formatted_table[, c("model", "formatted", "p.value")] #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   model                formatted        p.value #>   <chr>                <chr>            <chr>   #> 1 Unadjusted           1.21 (0.97–1.52) 0.093   #> 2 + Age                1.02 (0.80–1.28) 0.889   #> 3 + Tumour size        0.84 (0.66–1.07) 0.154   #> 4 + Grade              0.81 (0.63–1.04) 0.093   #> 5 + Lymph nodes        0.50 (0.38–0.65) <0.001  #> 6 + Oestrogen receptor 0.50 (0.38–0.65) <0.001

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: time--event outcomes supply model_type = "coxph" along time_var event_var:
code: result_cox <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data       = df,   outcome    = "death",   exposure   = "hormon",   covariates = covariates,   model_type = "coxph",   time_var   = "dtime",   event_var  = "death",   title      = "Effect of Hormonal Therapy on Survival (Rotterdam)" ) result_cox$plot

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: ggadjustedforest intentionally provide built-multi-outcome wrapper. outcome deserves carefully specified model, bundling single function call obscures . Instead, fit outcome separately stack plots patchwork, already dependency ggadjustedforest:  approach gives full control panel — different covariate sets, model types, axis scales per outcome. / operator stacks plots vertically; use | side--side.
code: library(patchwork)  p_death <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data = df, outcome = "death", exposure = "hormon",   covariates = covariates, model_type = "coxph",   time_var = "dtime", event_var = "death",   title = "Overall survival", show_table = FALSE )$plot  p_recur <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data = df, outcome = "recur", exposure = "hormon",   covariates = covariates, model_type = "coxph",   time_var = "rtime", event_var = "recur",   title = "Recurrence-free survival", show_table = FALSE )$plot  p_death / p_recur

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: major aesthetic parameters exposed:
code: gg_adjusted_forest(   data           = df,   outcome        = "death",   exposure       = "hormon",   covariates     = covariates,   model_type     = "coxph",   time_var       = "dtime",   event_var      = "death",   color          = "#2166ac",   point_size     = 5,   point_shape    = 18,   vline_color    = "firebrick",   vline_linetype = "dotted",   title          = "Custom aesthetics" )$plot

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: Use forest_table() need numbers:
code: forest_table(   data       = df,   outcome    = "death",   exposure   = "hormon",   covariates = covariates,   model_type = "logistic" ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   model      estimate ci        formatted        p.value     n #>   <chr>      <chr>    <chr>     <chr>            <chr>   <int> #> 1 Unadjusted 1.21     0.97–1.52 1.21 (0.97–1.52) 0.093    2982 #> 2 Adjusted   0.50     0.38–0.65 0.50 (0.38–0.65) <0.001   2982

title: Introduction to ggadjustedforest
text: Hernán MA, Robins JM (2020). Causal Inference: . Chapman & Hall/CRC. Royston P, Altman DG (2013). External validation Cox prognostic model: principles methods. BMC Med Res Methodol 13:33. (Rotterdam dataset) Vandenbroucke JP et al. (2007). Strengthening Reporting Observational Studies Epidemiology (STROBE). PLoS Med 4(10): e297. Westreich D, Greenland S (2013). table 2 fallacy: presenting interpreting confounder modifier coefficients. J Epidemiol 177(4): 292–298. ICH E9(R1) (2019). Statistical Principles Clinical Trials: Addendum Estimands Sensitivity Analysis Clinical Trials. ICH Harmonised Guideline.

what: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
title: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
text: ggadjustedforest creates publication-quality forest plots effect-size tables display unadjusted adjusted estimates user-specified exposure variable interest. Confounder coefficients hidden design, avoid “Table 2 fallcy” described Westreich & Greenland 2013, accordance causal inference principles.

title: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
text: estimand effect single exposure variable, reporting confounder coefficients alongside can misleading. coefficients identified causal model depend full causal structure. ggadjustedforest makes easy present exposure effect cleanly, adjustment, reducing susceptibility false misinterpretations confounder coefficients.

title: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
code: # Install from CRAN install.packages("ggadjustedforest")  # Or install the development version from GitHub # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("kriz98/gg_adjusted_forest")

title: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
text: examples use rotterdam breast cancer dataset survival package (2,982 patients; Rotterdam tumour bank). research question whether hormonal therapy (hormon) affects survival, adjusting age, tumour size, grade, lymph node involvement, oestrogen receptor level.
code: library(ggadjustedforest) library(dplyr) data(cancer, package = "survival")  df <- rotterdam |>   transmute(     hormon = hormon,   # 1 = hormonal therapy, 0 = none     age    = age,     size   = size,     # tumour size (mm)     grade  = grade,     nodes  = nodes,    # positive lymph nodes     er10   = er / 10,  # oestrogen receptor (fmol/10 l)     death  = death,     time   = dtime   ) |>   tidyr::drop_na()  covariates <- c("age", "size", "grade", "nodes", "er10")  # Cox proportional hazards — unadjusted vs adjusted result <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data       = df,   outcome    = "death",   exposure   = "hormon",   covariates = covariates,   model_type = "coxph",   time_var   = "time",   event_var  = "death",   title      = "Effect of Hormonal Therapy on Survival (Rotterdam)" ) result$plot result$table           # tibble of numeric estimates result$formatted_table # tibble with "HR (lower–upper)" strings  # Cumulative adjustment — watch the estimate evolve as confounders are added result_cum <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data       = df,   outcome    = "death",   exposure   = "hormon",   covariates = covariates,   model_type = "coxph",   time_var   = "time",   event_var  = "death",   cumulative = TRUE,   cumulative_labels = c(     "Unadjusted"                       = "Unadjusted",     "+ age"                            = "+ Age",     "+ age + size"                     = "+ Tumour size",     "+ age + size + grade"             = "+ Grade",     "+ age + size + grade + nodes"     = "+ Lymph nodes",     "+ age + size + grade + nodes + er10" = "+ Oestrogen receptor"   ),   title = "Cumulative Adjustment: Hormonal Therapy on Survival" ) result_cum$plot  # Multiple outcomes — stack with patchwork (already a dependency) library(patchwork) p1 <- gg_adjusted_forest(df, "death", "hormon", covariates,                           model_type = "coxph", time_var = "time",                           event_var = "death", title = "Overall survival",                           show_table = FALSE)$plot p2 <- gg_adjusted_forest(df, "death", "nodes", covariates[covariates != "nodes"],                           model_type = "coxph", time_var = "time",                           event_var = "death", title = "Effect of lymph node burden",                           show_table = FALSE)$plot p1 / p2

title: Publication Ready Forest Plots for Estimand of Interest
text: Westreich D, Greenland S (2013). table 2 fallacy. J Epidemiol 177(4): 292–298. Hernán MA, Robins JM (2020). Causal Inference: . Chapman & Hall/CRC. ICH E9(R1) (2019). Addendum Estimands.

what: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
title: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
text: convenience wrapper around gg_adjusted_forest() returns formatted table. Useful want numeric summaries without producing graphic.

title: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
code: forest_table(   data,   outcome,   exposure,   covariates = NULL,   model_type = "logistic",   cumulative = FALSE,   cumulative_labels = NULL,   conf_level = 0.95,   time_var = NULL,   event_var = NULL,   strata = NULL,   cluster = NULL,   weights = NULL,   table_digits = 2 )

title: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
text: data data frame containing variables. outcome Character string. Name outcome variable (ignored Cox models - use time_var event_var instead). exposure Character string. Name exposure variable interest. covariates Character vector confounder/covariate names. non-cumulative mode covariates added together; cumulative mode added one time order supplied. Default NULL produces unadjusted estimate. model_type Character. One "logistic" (default), "linear", "poisson", "coxph". cumulative Logical. TRUE, fit models progressively add one covariate time show step separate row. Default FALSE. Important: Odds ratios ("logistic") hazard ratios ("coxph") non-collapsible effect measures. means exposure coefficient change covariates added even complete absence confounding, adding covariates reduces residual variance latent scale. Consequently, shifting HR across sequential models cleanly attributed confounding. causal inference, unadjusted vs. fully-adjusted comparison (default) preferred. Cumulative display interpretable collapsible measures: risk differences ("linear") risk ratios ("poisson"). cumulative_labels Optional named character vector rename cumulative model labels. Names match auto-generated labels (e.g., "+ age", "+ age + sex"); values replacement labels. conf_level Numeric. Confidence level intervals. Default 0.95. time_var Character. Name time variable (Cox model ). event_var Character. Name event indicator variable (Cox model ; 0/1 logical). strata Character. Name stratification variable Cox models. Default NULL. cluster Character. Name clustering variable cluster-robust standard errors. Requires sandwich lmtest packages. Default NULL. weights Character. Name survey/frequency weight variable. Default NULL. table_digits Integer. Number decimal places table. Default 2.

title: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
text: data frame columns: model Row label (e.g., "Unadjusted", "Adjusted"). estimate Point estimate (formatted character). ci Confidence interval character string (e.g., "0.95–1.42"). formatted Combined estimate CI (e.g., "1.15 (0.95–1.42)"). p.value Formatted p-value character string. n Number observations.

title: Extract a formatted effect-size table without generating a plot — forest_table
code: data(mtcars) mtcars$am <- as.integer(mtcars$am) forest_table(   data       = mtcars,   outcome    = "am",   exposure   = "hp",   covariates = c("wt", "cyl"),   model_type = "logistic" ) #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   model      estimate ci        formatted        p.value     n #>   <chr>      <chr>    <chr>     <chr>            <chr>   <int> #> 1 Unadjusted 0.99     0.98–1.00 0.99 (0.98–1.00) 0.181      32 #> 2 Adjusted   1.03     1.00–1.09 1.03 (1.00–1.09) 0.084      32

what: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
title: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
text: Creates publication-quality forest plot showing unadjusted adjusted (cumulatively adjusted) effect estimates specified exposure variable, hiding confounder coefficients accordance causal inference principles.

title: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
code: gg_adjusted_forest(   data,   outcome,   exposure,   covariates = NULL,   model_type = "logistic",   cumulative = FALSE,   cumulative_labels = NULL,   effect_label = NULL,   title = NULL,   ref_line = NULL,   point_size = 4,   point_shape = 15,   line_size = 0.7,   color = "black",   colour = NULL,   vline_color = "grey50",   vline_linetype = "dashed",   x_limits = NULL,   x_breaks = NULL,   log_scale = TRUE,   conf_level = 0.95,   time_var = NULL,   event_var = NULL,   strata = NULL,   cluster = NULL,   weights = NULL,   show_table = TRUE,   table_digits = 2 )

title: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
text: data data frame containing variables. outcome Character string. Name outcome variable (ignored Cox models - use time_var event_var instead). exposure Character string. Name exposure variable interest. covariates Character vector confounder/covariate names. non-cumulative mode covariates added together; cumulative mode added one time order supplied. Default NULL produces unadjusted estimate. model_type Character. One "logistic" (default), "linear", "poisson", "coxph". cumulative Logical. TRUE, fit models progressively add one covariate time show step separate row. Default FALSE. Important: Odds ratios ("logistic") hazard ratios ("coxph") non-collapsible effect measures. means exposure coefficient change covariates added even complete absence confounding, adding covariates reduces residual variance latent scale. Consequently, shifting HR across sequential models cleanly attributed confounding. causal inference, unadjusted vs. fully-adjusted comparison (default) preferred. Cumulative display interpretable collapsible measures: risk differences ("linear") risk ratios ("poisson"). cumulative_labels Optional named character vector rename cumulative model labels. Names match auto-generated labels (e.g., "+ age", "+ age + sex"); values replacement labels. effect_label Character. X-axis label. Defaults "Odds Ratio (95 \% CI)" logistic, "Risk Ratio (95 \% CI)" Poisson, "Hazard Ratio (95 \% CI)" Cox, "Coefficient (95 \% CI)" linear. title Character. Plot title. Default NULL (title). ref_line Numeric. Position vertical reference line. Defaults 1 ratio models 0 linear. point_size Numeric. Size point estimate symbol. Default 4. point_shape Integer. ggplot2 shape code. Default 15 (filled square). line_size Numeric. Thickness CI lines. Default 0.7. color Character. Colour points CI lines. Default "black". colour Alias color (British English spelling). vline_color Character. Colour reference line. Default "grey50". vline_linetype Character. Linetype reference line. Default "dashed". x_limits Numeric vector length 2. Manual x-axis limits. Default NULL (automatic). x_breaks Numeric vector. Manual x-axis break positions. Default NULL (automatic). log_scale Logical. Use log scale x-axis ratio models. Default TRUE. conf_level Numeric. Confidence level intervals. Default 0.95. time_var Character. Name time variable (Cox model ). event_var Character. Name event indicator variable (Cox model ; 0/1 logical). strata Character. Name stratification variable Cox models. Default NULL. cluster Character. Name clustering variable cluster-robust standard errors. Requires sandwich lmtest packages. Default NULL. weights Character. Name survey/frequency weight variable. Default NULL. show_table Logical. Combine forest plot formatted table panel (using patchwork). Default TRUE. table_digits Integer. Number decimal places table. Default 2.

title: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
text: object class ggadjustedforest (list) components: plot combined ggplot2/patchwork plot object. show_table = FALSE just forest plot. table data frame columns model, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value, n. formatted_table data frame formatted column containing strings like "1.23 (1.01-1.55)".

title: Forest plot of unadjusted and adjusted effects for a single exposure — gg_adjusted_forest
code: # Logistic regression example data(mtcars) mtcars$am <- as.integer(mtcars$am)   # binary outcome result <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data        = mtcars,   outcome     = "am",   exposure    = "hp",   covariates  = c("wt", "cyl"),   model_type  = "logistic",   title       = "Effect of Horsepower on Transmission Type" ) #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred result$table #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #>   model      estimate conf.low conf.high p.value     n #>   <fct>         <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> <int> #> 1 Unadjusted    0.992    0.979      1.00  0.181     32 #> 2 Adjusted      1.03     1.00       1.09  0.0840    32  # Cumulative adjustment result2 <- gg_adjusted_forest(   data       = mtcars,   outcome    = "am",   exposure   = "hp",   covariates = c("wt", "cyl"),   cumulative = TRUE ) #> Warning: Cumulative adjustment with `model_type = "logistic"` uses a non-collapsible effect measure (odds ratio). #> The exposure coefficient will change across sequential models even without confounding, because adding covariates reduces residual variance on the latent scale. Changes in the OR/HR cannot be cleanly attributed to confounding. For causal inference, prefer the default unadjusted vs. fully-adjusted display. Cumulative mode is most interpretable for collapsible measures: risk differences (model_type = "linear") or risk ratios (model_type = "poisson"). #> Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically title: ggadjustedforest 0.1.1
text: cumulative = TRUE model_type = "logistic" "coxph" now emits warning explaining odds ratios hazard ratios non-collapsible effect measures. shifting /HR across sequential models cleanly attributed confounding; cumulative display interpretable collapsible measures (risk differences, risk ratios). feature retained descriptive predictive modelling contexts.

title: ggadjustedforest 0.1.1
text: Updated vignette README examples use rotterdam breast cancer dataset (survival) Cox model demonstration. Installation instructions updated reflect CRAN availability. Long plot titles now wrap automatically prevent clipping.

title: ggadjustedforest 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-07-17

title: ggadjustedforest 0.1.0
text: gg_adjusted_forest(): main function producing forest plots tables unadjusted adjusted exposure effects. forest_table(): standalone table extraction helper. Supports "logistic", "linear", "poisson", "coxph" model types. Cumulative adjustment mode (cumulative = TRUE). Optional cluster-robust standard errors via sandwich / lmtest. Optional survey weights. patchwork-based combined plot + table display. S3 print plot methods ggadjustedforest objects.
